name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْنَـٰهَا وَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهَا حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
A sign for them is the dead earth, which We revive and bring forth grain out of it, so they eat of it. (33)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ وَفَجَّرْنَا فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْعُيُونِ (٣٤)
```

```english
We make in it orchards of date palms and vines, and We cause springs to gush forth in it, (34)
```

```arabic
لِيَأْكُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦ وَمَا عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
so that they may eat of its fruit and what their hands have cultivated. Will they not then give thanks? (35)
```

```arabic
سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
Immaculate is He who has created all the kinds of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know. (36)
```

```arabic
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فَإِذَا هُم مُّظْلِمُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
A sign for them is the night, which We strip of daylight, and, behold, they find themselves in the dark! (37)
```

```arabic
وَٱلشَّمْسُ تَجْرِى لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَّهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٣٨)
```

```english
The sun runs on to its place of rest: That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (38)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْنَـٰهُ مَنَازِلَ حَتَّىٰ عَادَ كَٱلْعُرْجُونِ ٱلْقَدِيمِ (٣٩)
```

```english
As for the moon, We have ordained its phases, until it becomes like an old palm leaf. (39)
```

```arabic
لَا ٱلشَّمْسُ يَنۢبَغِى لَهَآ أَن تُدْرِكَ ٱلْقَمَرَ وَلَا ٱلَّيْلُ سَابِقُ ٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
Neither it behooves the sun to overtake the moon, nor may the night outrun the day, and each swims in an orbit. (40)
```

```arabic
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (٤١)
```

```english
A sign for them is that We carried their progeny in the laden ship, (41)
```

```arabic
وَخَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ مَا يَرْكَبُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
and We have created for them what is similar to it, which they ride. (42)
```

```arabic
وَإِن نَّشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلَا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنقَذُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
And if We like We drown them, whereat they have no one to call for help, nor are they rescued (43)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٤٤)
```

```english
except by a mercy from Us and for an enjoyment until some time. (44)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَمَا خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
And when they are told, ‘Beware of that which is before you and that which is behind you, so that you may receive [His] mercy…’ (45)
```

```arabic
وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
There does not come to them any sign from among the signs of their Lord but that they have been disregarding it. (46)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنُطْعِمُ مَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُۥٓ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٧)
```

```english
When they are told, ‘Spend out of what Allah has provided you,’ the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Shall we feed [someone] whom Allah would feed, if He wished? You are only in manifest error.’ (47)
```

## (Commentary)

After relating to them the story of the People of the Town and the fate that befell them due to their polytheism (الشرك) and their denial of the messengers, and after rebuking them for belittling the matter of the message and warning them of the descent of punishment upon them—just as it descended upon the deniers of former generations—and informing them that they shall all be brought forth for reckoning and recompense;

He now presents signs (آيات) from creation and governance (التدبير) that point to His Lordship (الربوبية) and His Divinity (الألوهية), the Exalted, alone without any partner. Then He rebukes them for failing to reflect upon the signs of Oneness (الوحدانية) and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the final destination of souls* — and for turning away from them, mocking the Truth, and withholding spending (الإنفاق) for the poor and the destitute.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ الْأَرْضُ الْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْناها وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ** — *A sign for them is the dead earth: We revive it and bring forth from it grain, so they eat of it.* (36:33). In this verse and the two following it, the Glorified mentions a sign from the signs of Lordship (الربوبية), which is the governance of the matter of people's sustenance and their nourishment from the fruits of plants, including grains, dates, grapes, and others.

Thus, His saying: **وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ الْأَرْضُ الْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْناها** — *A sign for them is the dead earth: We revive it* — although its apparent meaning is that the sign is the earth itself, these two clauses serve as a preamble to His saying: **وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا** — *and bring forth from it grain* — etc. They are directed toward indicating that these plant-based foods are among the effects of breathing life into the dead earth and transforming it into grain and fruit from which they eat. Therefore, the sign, upon reflection, is the dead earth in terms of the manifestation of these properties within it and the completion of the governance of people's sustenance through it.

His saying: **وَأَخْرَجْنا مِنْها حَبًّا** — *and bring forth from it grain* — means: We brought forth from the earth, by causing plants to grow, grain such as wheat, barley, rice, and other legumes.

His saying: **فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ** — *so they eat of it* — is a consequence of bringing forth the grain, for it is through eating that the governance is completed. The pronoun in **فَمِنْهُ** (of it) refers to the grain.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْنا فِيها جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَأَعْنابٍ وَفَجَّرْنا فِيها مِنَ الْعُيُونِ** — *We have made in it orchards of palms and vines, and We have caused springs to gush forth in it,* (36:34). Al-Raghib said: "The garden (الجنة) is every orchard possessing trees that cover the ground with their foliage." End quote. Date-palms (النخيل) is the plural of *nakhl*, which is well-known. Grapes (الأعناب) is the plural of *inab*, a term applied both to the tree—which is the vine—and to the fruit.

Al-Raghib said: "The eye (العين) is the organ of sight..." until he said: "The term *ayn* is used metaphorically for various meanings found in the organ of sight from different perspectives..." until he said: "And the source of water is called an *ayn* (spring) by way of comparison to it, because of the water it contains." End quote. Gushing (التفجير) in the earth is the cleaving of it to bring forth water. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَأْكُلُوا مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ** — *that they may eat of its fruits and what their hands have not wrought. Will they not then be grateful?* (36:35). The *lam* (لـ) is to explain the cause of what was mentioned in the previous verse; that is: We made gardens therein and caused springs to gush forth by cleaving the earth so that people may eat of its fruit.

Regarding His saying: **مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ** — *of its fruit* — it has been said that the pronoun refers to that which was "made" (المجعول) of the gardens, which is why it is singular and masculine, rather than saying *min thamariha* (of their fruit, i.e., the gardens) or *min thamarihima* (of the fruit of both, i.e., the palms and vines).

It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the mentioned subject, and sometimes a pronoun functions like a demonstrative pronoun (اسم الإشارة), as in the poem of Ru'bah:
*In it are lines of black and white,*
*As if it (ka-annahu) were the variegation of vitiligo on the skin.*
It is narrated that Abu Ubaydah asked him about his saying *ka-annahu* (as if it), and he replied: "It was that (dhalika)."

There are other poor opinions regarding the reference of the pronoun in **مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ** (of its fruit), such as the view of some that the pronoun refers only to the date-palms; or the view that it refers to the water because the "springs" indicate it, or by assuming a suppressed possessive (مضاف) where the intended meaning is "the water of the springs"; or the view that the pronoun refers to the "gushing" (التفجير) understood from **فَجَّرْنا** (We caused to gush), with "fruit" in these two views meaning "benefit"; or the view that the pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, and its attribution to Him is because He created it and owns it.

Regarding His saying: **وَما عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ** — *and what their hands have not wrought* — work (العمل) is action (الفعل). The difference between them—according to what Al-Raghib mentioned—is that "work" is most often used for an action accompanied by intent and will. For this reason, its use for animals and inanimate objects is rare. Also for this reason, "work" is described as righteous (الصلاح) or otherwise; thus one says "a righteous deed" (عمل صالح) or "an evil deed" (عمل طالح), whereas absolute "action" is not so described.

The *ma* (ما) in **وَما عَمِلَتْهُ** is negating (نافية), and the meaning is: "And their hands did not produce the fruit such that they would be partners with Us in the governance (تدبير) of sustenance; rather, it is among that which We have uniquely created and through which We completed the governance without seeking their assistance. So why is it that they do not give thanks?"

This meaning is supported by His saying at the end of the Surah, where He reminds them of His favor in creating livestock for the governance of their sustenance and life: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا خَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا أَنْعاماً** — *Have they not seen that We have created for them cattle out of the handiwork of Our hands,* — until He says — **وَمِنْها يَأْكُلُونَ وَلَهُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ وَمَشارِبُ أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ** — *and of them they eat. There are [other] profits for them in them and drinks. Will they not then be grateful?* (36:71-73).

Some have considered the possibility that the *ma* (ما) in **وَما عَمِلَتْهُ** is a relative pronoun (موصولة) conjoined to "its fruit" (ثمره). The meaning would then be: "That they may eat of its fruit and of that which their hands have wrought from its fruit, such as vinegar and syrup extracted from dates, grapes, and so on."

This view, although some consider it more plausible than the previous one, is not quite correct. This is because the context is one of explaining signs (آيات) that point to His Lordship, the Exalted, by mentioning matters of governance (التدبير) that belong exclusively to Him. It is not suitable to mention any part of another's governance alongside His when establishing the proof. If the intent were to mention their work insofar as it traces back to His creation and is part of the general governance, it would have been more appropriate to say: "And what We guided them to work," or something conveying that meaning, to negate any illusion of partnership in governance.

Others have suggested that *ma* (ما) is an indefinite noun described by a clause (نكرة موصوفة) conjoined to "its fruit." The meaning would be: "That they may eat of its fruit and of something their hands have wrought." The same objection raised against the previous view applies here as well.

His saying: **أَفَلا يَشْكُرُونَ** — *Will they not then be grateful?* — is a rebuke and a denunciation of their lack of gratitude. Their gratitude to Him, the Exalted, for this governance consists of manifesting the beauty of His blessings by mentioning Him in word and deed—that is, by manifesting that they are His servants, governed by His governance, which is worship (العبادة). Thus, gratitude to Him, the Exalted, is the recognition of His Lordship and taking Him as the God to be worshipped.

His saying, the Exalted: **سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — *Immaculate is He who has created all the pairs, of what the earth grows, and of themselves, and of what they do not know.* (36:36). This is an instantiation of His glorification (تنزيهه), the Exalted. Since He mentioned their lack of gratitude to Him for what He created for them of various plants and the sustenance He provided of grains and fruits—and He only brought that about by pairing some plants with others, as He said: **وَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ** — *and We have caused every delightful kind [of plant] to grow in it* (50:7)—He pointed to something even greater and more expansive than the creation of plant pairs. This is the creation of all pairs (الأزواج) and the organization of the witnessed world by the procreation of everything from an active (فاعل) and a passive (منفعل) element before it, which are its two parents, like the male and female among humans, animals, and plants. Every active and passive element that meet produce a third matter by their meeting. He, the Exalted, pointed to this and glorified Himself by saying: **سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها** — *Immaculate is He who has created all the pairs* — etc. Thus, His saying: **سُبْحانَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها** is an instantiation of glorification (تسبيح) as dictated by the context, not merely a report.

His saying: **مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ** — *of what the earth grows* — and what follows it is an elucidation of the "pairs." That which the earth grows is the plants, and it is not far-fetched that it includes animals as well. Indeed, He, the Exalted, said regarding man—who is a species of animal: **وَاللهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً** — *And Allah made you grow from the earth [like a] plant* (71:17). This is supported by the fact that the apparent context of the elucidation (البيان) is to encompass the subject being elucidated (المبين), yet animals were not mentioned in the enumeration of pairs.

His saying: **وَمِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ** — *and of themselves* — meaning human beings. And His saying: **وَمِمَّا لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — *and of what they do not know* — which refers to that part of creation which man is ignorant of, or ignorant of the manner of its appearance or the appearance of multiplicity within it.

It has sometimes been said regarding the verse that "pairs" (الأزواج) refers to types and categories. However, this is not supported by the verses that mention the creation of pairs, such as His saying: **وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition.* (51:49). Pairing and a kind of harmony and composition are among the necessary implications of the concept of "pair-ness" (الزوجية).

Al-Raghib said: "The term *zawj* (pair/spouse) is applied to each one of a couple, male and female, among mating animals. It is also applied to each of a pair in other things, such as a pair of boots or sandals. It is also applied to everything that is coupled with another, whether similar to it or its opposite." He said: "And His saying: **خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ** (We created pairs) explains that everything in the world is a pair insofar as it has an opposite, a like, or a composition; indeed, it is never devoid of composition in some way." End quote.

Thus, the "pair-ness" of a pair is its being dependent for its realization upon harmony and composition. Therefore, each of the two companions, insofar as they are companions, is called a *zawj* (pair) because of its need for its companion. Likewise, the sum of the two companions is called a *zawj* because of its need for harmony and composition to be realized as a pair. Thus, things being "pairs" means the coupling of some with others to produce a third, or its being generated from the harmony of two.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَآيَةٌ لَهُمُ اللَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ النَّهارَ فَإِذا هُمْ مُظْلِمُونَ** — *A sign for them is the night: We strip the day of it, and lo! they are plunged in darkness.* (36:37). This is another sign from the signs of Lordship pointing to the occurrence of the general celestial governance (التدبير) for the human world, mentioned across four verses.

There is no doubt that the sign points to the sudden onset of night following the departure of day. Stripping (السلخ) in the verse carries the meaning of "extracting" (الإخراج), which is why it is made transitive with the preposition *min* (from). If it carried the meaning of "removing" (النزع), as in our saying: "I stripped the hide from (an) the sheep," it would necessarily be transitive with *an* rather than *min*.

This is supported by the fact that He, the Exalted, in various places in His speech, describes the arrival of each of the night and day after the other as an "interweaving" (إيلاج) of one into the other. He said in several places: **يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ** — *He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night.* (22:61). If the arrival of day after night is an interweaving of day into night conceptually, then the sudden onset of night after day is an extraction of day from night conceptually.

It is as if the night had covered them and its darkness surrounded them, then the day interwoven into it, so its light and radiance encompassed them; then it exited from it, and the night suddenly came upon them again by the closing in of darkness and its surrounding what the day had illuminated. Thus, there is a kind of metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية) in the speech.

Perhaps what we have mentioned of this perspective is sufficient to replace the lengthy discussions they have engaged in regarding the meaning of "stripping the day from the night" and the subsequent sudden onset of night.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ** — *And the sun runs on to its resting place. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing.* (36:38). Its "running" (جريها) is its motion. His saying **لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها** — *to its resting place* — the *lam* (لـ) carries the meaning of "toward" (إلى) or indicates the end-goal (الغاية). The "resting place" (المستقر) is a mim-infinitve (مصدر ميمي) or a noun of time or place. The meaning is that it moves toward its resting place or until it reaches its resting place—that is, its stability and stillness by the expiration of its term, or the time of its stability, or its location.

As for its "running," which is its motion, the apparent sensory observation establishes for it a circular motion around the earth. However, scientific research dictates the opposite and reveals that it, along with its planets, has a translational motion toward the constellation Lyra (النسر الواقع).

In any case, the sum of the meaning is that the sun continues to run as long as the worldly system remains as it is, until it settles and becomes still by the expiration of its term, whereupon the world will be destroyed and this system will be nullified. This meaning ultimately returns to the meaning of the recitation attributed to the People of the House (أهل البيت) and others: "And the sun runs, having no resting place for it" (لا مستقر لها), as has been said.

As for interpreting its "running" as its rotational motion around its center, this is contrary to the apparent meaning of "running" (الجري), which indicates translation from one place to another.

His saying: **ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ** — *That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing* — means that the aforementioned running is an ordaining and a governance from One who is not overcome by any overcomer in His will, nor is He ignorant of the aspects of benefit in His acts.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْناهُ مَنازِلَ حَتَّى عادَ كَالْعُرْجُونِ الْقَدِيمِ** — *As for the moon, We have ordained mansions for it, until it becomes like an old palm-stalk.* (36:39). Mansions (المنازل) is the plural of *manzil*, a noun of place derived from "descending" (النزول). It is apparent that what is intended are the twenty-eight mansions that the moon traverses in approximately every twenty-eight days and nights.

The palm-stalk (العرجون) is the stem of the date-cluster from the branchlets to its base; it is a yellow, curved stem that resembles the crescent moon. Old (القديم) means ancient.

Views have differed regarding the meaning of the verse due to the difference in its grammatical structure. The closest interpretation to understanding is the view of those who say the estimation is: "And the moon, We have ordained it as possessing mansions," or "We have ordained mansions for it until it returns as a crescent resembling an ancient, yellowed palm-stalk."

The verse points to the varying appearances of the moon relative to the inhabitants of the earth. Its light is acquired from the sun; approximately half of its sphere is illuminated by it, while the other half, not facing the sun, is dark. Then the position of illumination changes and continues to do so until it returns to the first position. This results in it appearing to the inhabitants of the earth in the form of a crescent, then the light continues to spread over it until it becomes a full moon, then it continues to decrease until it returns to what it was at its beginning.

The variation of its forms has prominent effects on land and sea and the life of people, as explained in relevant research.

Thus, the noble verse mentions, as a sign of the moon, its states that occur relative to the earth and its people, rather than its state in itself or its state relative to the sun only.

From here, it is not far-fetched to say regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها** — *And the sun runs on to its resting place* — that the intent of His saying **تَجْرِي** (runs) is an allusion to what the apparent sense gives of its daily, seasonal, and annual motion, which is its state relative to us; and by His saying **لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها** (to its resting place), its state in itself is intended, which is its stillness relative to its planets moving around it. It is as if it were said: "And a sign for them is that the sun, despite its stability, runs for them, and the All-mighty, the All-knowing has thereby governed the existence of the terrestrial world and the life of its inhabitants." And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَا الشَّمْسُ يَنْبَغِي لَها أَنْ تُدْرِكَ الْقَمَرَ وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ وَكُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ** — *Neither it behooves the sun to overtake the moon, nor may the night outrun the day, and each swims in an orbit.* (36:40). The word *yanbaghi* (behooves/is possible) indicates preference, and the negation of the preference of overtaking for the sun is a negation of its occurrence from it. What is intended is that the governance is not something that runs one day and stops another; rather, it is a permanent governance, neither disordered nor nullified, until the term appointed by Him, the Exalted, for that purpose expires.

The meaning is that the sun and the moon are constant in the course mapped out for them; thus, the sun does not overtake the moon such that the governance carried out by them would be disordered. Nor does the night outrun the day, for they alternate in governance; it does not happen that the night precedes the day such that two nights join together, then two days, but rather they alternate.

He did not mention the negation of the moon overtaking the sun, nor the negation of the day outrunning the night, because the context is one of explaining the preservation of the divine order from disorder and corruption. Thus, He negated the overtaking of that which is greater and stronger—the sun—of that which is smaller and weaker—the moon; from this, the reverse case is understood. He also negated the outrunning of the night, which is the absence of the day that follows it, for the night is naturally subsequent to it; from this, the reverse case is also understood.

His saying: **وَكُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ** — *and each swims in an orbit* — means that each of the sun, the moon, and others among the stars and planets runs in a course specific to it, just as a fish swims in water. Thus, the orbit (الفلك) is the spatial path in which the celestial body moves. It is not far-fetched then that "each" (كل) refers to each of the sun, the moon, the night, and the day, even if there is nothing in His speech, the Exalted, to testify to that.

The use of the plural pronoun specific to rational beings in His saying **يَسْبَحُونَ** (they swim) is perhaps to indicate their compliance with His will and their obedience to His command, the Exalted, like rational beings, as in His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى إِلَى السَّماءِ وَهِيَ دُخانٌ فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ** — *Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly.’* (41:11).

The commentators have other unsettled opinions regarding the sentences of this verse, which we have omitted; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the detailed works.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَآيَةٌ لَهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ** — *A sign for them is that We carried their progeny in the laden ship,* (36:41). Al-Raghib said: "Progeny (الذرية) originally refers to small children; in common usage, it applies to both small and large together. It is used for the singular and the plural, though its origin is for the plural." End quote. The ship (الفلك) is the vessel, and laden (المشحون) means filled.

This is another sign from the signs of His Lordship, the Exalted, which is the operation of His governance in the sea, where He carries their progeny in the ship laden with them and their goods, crossing with it from one side to another for trade and other purposes. There is no carrier for them therein, nor any protector for them from drowning, except Him, the Exalted. The properties they benefit from in seafaring are matters subjected to Him, the Exalted, tracing back to His creation; moreover, these causes, if they did not trace back to Him, the Exalted, would be of no avail.

The carrying was attributed to the "progeny" rather than themselves—He did not say "We carried them"—to stir compassion and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَخَلَقْنا لَهُمْ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ** — *and We have created for them the like of it which they ride.* (36:42). What is intended by it—according to what they have interpreted—is livestock. He, the Exalted, said: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما يَرْكَبُونَ** — *and He has made for you ships and cattle that you ride,* (43:12), and He said: **وَعَلَيْها وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ** — *and you are carried on them and on ships.* (40:80).

Some have interpreted the "ship" mentioned in the previous verse as the Ark of Noah (peace be upon him), and what is in this verse as the ships and boats made after it; this is a poor interpretation. Similar to it is the interpretation of what is in this verse as camels specifically.

Perhaps what is in this verse can be interpreted as airplanes and airships made in these times; generalization is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ نَشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْقَذُونَ** — *And if We wish, We drown them: then they have no one to help them, nor are they rescued,* (36:43). The helper (الصريخ) is the one who answers the cry and aids the one seeking help. Rescuing (الإنقاذ) is saving from drowning.

The verse is connected to His previous saying: **أَنَّا حَمَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ** — *that We carried their progeny in the laden ship* — meaning: the matter is subject to Our will; if We wish, We drown them, and no helper shall aid them, nor shall any rescuer save them.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَمَتاعاً إِلى حِينٍ** — *except as a mercy from Us and an enjoyment for a while.* (36:44). This is an elliptical exception (استثناء مفرغ), and the estimation is: they are not saved by any cause or any matter except due to a mercy from Us that reaches them, and for enjoyment until the time of the appointed term We have ordained for them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ** — *And when they are told, ‘Beware of what is before you and what is behind you, so that you may receive [His] mercy...’* (36:45). Since He mentioned the signs pointing to Lordship, He censured them for not observing its right, not turning toward it, and not acting upon its implications. So when it is said to them: "These clear signs speak that your Lord is Allah, so beware of disobeying Him in your present state and the sins you have previously committed," or "the punishment for polytheism and sins with which you are afflicted and what you have left behind you," or "beware of what is before you of polytheism and sins in the life of this world and what is behind you of punishment in the Hereafter," they turned away from it and did not respond to it, as is their habit with all the signs they are reminded of.

From here, it becomes clear, firstly: that what is intended by "what is before them and what is behind them" is the polytheism and sins they are afflicted with in their present state and what they were afflicted with before; or the punishment they have deserved thereby—and the outcome is the same; or polytheism and sins in this world and punishment in the Hereafter, which is the most plausible of views.

Secondly: that the omission of the apodosis (جواب) of *idha* (when) is to indicate that their state has reached such a level of audacity against Allah and belittling of the Truth that it is impossible to mention how they would answer the caller to Truth when he calls them to godwariness (التقوى); thus it must be left out of sorrow and not mentioned. This has been indicated by His saying: **وَما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ** — *Not a sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord but that they are disregardful of it.* (36:46).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ** — *Not a sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord but that they are disregardful of it.* (36:46). What is intended by the "coming" of the signs is their reaching them through observation or through recitation and mention. Also, it is more general than being a sign in the horizons or within the souls, or being a miraculous sign like the Qur'an; they are disregardful of all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ** — *And when they are told, ‘Spend out of what Allah has provided you,’* (36:47) until the end of the verse. His saying: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ** dealt with their response when called to the worship of Allah, which is one of the two pillars of the true religion. This verse deals with their response when called to compassion for the creation of Allah, which is the other pillar. It is known that their response is rejection rather than acceptance.

Thus, His saying: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ** includes a call to them to spend on the poor and the destitute from their wealth. In expressing wealth as "what Allah has provided you," there is an intimation that the true owner of it is Allah, who provided it to them and gave them authority over it. He is the one who created the poor and the destitute and established their need for the surplus provisions these people possess, which they do not lack. So let them spend on them and act with excellence and beauty, for Allah loves excellence (الإحسان) and beautiful action.

His saying: **قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنُطْعِمُ مَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ أَطْعَمَهُ** — *the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Shall we feed him whom Allah would have fed, had He wished?* (36:47) is their response to the call to spend. The speaker was explicitly mentioned as "those who disbelieved" (الذين كفروا)—whereas the context would normally require a pronoun—to indicate that their disbelief in the Truth and their turning away from it by following desires is what prompted them to offer such an excuse, based on turning away from what the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — calls for of compassion for Allah's creation and rectifying what is corrupted in society. Likewise, the explicit mention in His saying "to those who believed" (للذين آمنوا) is to indicate that the ones who said "Spend out of what Allah has provided you" were the believers.

In their saying: **أَنُطْعِمُ مَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ أَطْعَمَهُ** — *Shall we feed him whom Allah would have fed, had He wished?* — there is an intimation that the believers only said to them: "Spend out of what Allah has provided you" on the grounds that it is something Allah wishes and desires as a religious ruling. So they rejected it by arguing that Allah's will does not fail to achieve its object; thus, if He wished to feed them, He would have fed them—that is, He would have increased their sustenance and made them wealthy.

This is a fallacy (مغالطة) on their part, in which they confused the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية)—which is based on trial, examination, and guiding servants to what contains the benefit of their state in their world and their Hereafter, and which may fail to achieve its object through disobedience—with the creative will (الإرادة التكوينية), which never fails to achieve its object. It is well known that Allah's wish and will concerning feeding the poor and spending on them is of the legislative wish, not the creative one. Thus, its failure to be realized in the case of the poor only points to the disobedience of those who disbelieved and their rebellion against what they were commanded, not to the lack of the will's attachment to it or the falsehood of the one claiming it.

This is a fallacy upon which they built most of the pagan traditions they fabricated. Allah, the Glorified, has related this from them in His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — *The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him.’* (16:35); and His saying: **سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — *The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partners [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’* (6:148); and His saying: **وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ** — *They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’* (43:20).

His saying: **إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ** — *You are only in manifest error.* (36:47) is part of the speech of those who disbelieved, addressing the believers; meaning: "You are in manifest error in your claim that Allah commanded us to spend and wished that from us."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin, Abu Ja'far al-Baqir, and Ja'far al-Sadiq (peace be upon them): **لا مستقر لها** (having no resting place for it) with the *ra* in the accusative case.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abu Dharr, who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَالشَّمْسُ تَجْرِي لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَها** (And the sun runs on to its resting place). He said: 'Its resting place is beneath the Throne.'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated from Abu Dharr from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, in both brief and lengthy versions. In some of them, it is mentioned that after sunset, it ascends heaven by heaven until it reaches beneath the Throne, where it prostrates and seeks permission to rise, remaining so until it is clothed in light and permitted to rise.

If the narration is authentic, it must be interpreted figuratively.

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain to Sallam ibn al-Mustanir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created the sun before the moon, and He created light before darkness."

In *al-Majma'*, al-Ayyashi narrated in his *Tafsir* with a chain to al-Ash'ath ibn Hatim, who said: "I was in Khurasan when al-Rida (peace be upon him), al-Fadl ibn Sahl, and al-Ma'mun gathered in the pavilion at Marw. The table was set, and al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'A man from the Children of Israel asked me in Medina, saying: Was the day created before or the night? What do you have to say?' He said: 'They discussed the matter, but they had nothing to say regarding it.'"

"Then al-Fadl said to al-Rida: 'Inform us of it, may Allah rectify you.' He said: 'Yes. From the Qur'an or from calculation?' Al-Fadl said to him: 'From the perspective of calculation.' He said: 'You know, O Fadl, that the ascendant of the world is Cancer, and the planets were in their positions of exaltation: Saturn in Libra, Jupiter in Cancer, Mars in Capricorn, the Sun in Aries, Venus in Pisces, Mercury in Virgo, and the Moon in Taurus. Thus, the sun would be in the tenth house, the midheaven; so the day is before the night. And from the Qur'an is His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ** (nor may the night outrun the day), meaning the night has been preceded by the day.'"

I say: Al-Alusi quoted this hadith in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*, then said: "In the inference from the verse, there is an obvious discussion. As for calculation, it has a basis in general, and the astronomers held that the beginning of the cycle is the meridian circle, which accords with what was mentioned. What is most likely is that the report is not authentic from its origin, for al-Rida is too exalted to infer from the verse what you have heard of his claim." End quote.

The matter became confused for him in grasping the reality of the meaning of night and day.

To clarify: Night and day are opposites in the sense of privation and possession (العدم والملكة), like blindness and sight. Just as blindness is not the absolute absence of sight—such that a wall, for instance, would be blind because of the absence of sight in it—but rather it is the absence of sight in that which is naturally disposed to possess sight, like a human being; likewise, night is not the absolute absence of light. Rather, it is the time of the absence of illumination of a region of the earth by the light of the sun. It is well known that the realization of privation (عدم الملكة) depends on the prior realization of the corresponding possession (الملكة) so that it may be defined in relation to it. If there were no sight, blindness would not be realized; and if there were no day, night would not be realized.

Thus, absolute night, in the sense by which it is night, is preceded in existence by day. His saying: **وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ** (nor may the night outrun the day), although it looks at the assumed order between the days and nights—that there is a day, then a night, then a day, then a night—and that none of these nights precedes the day adjacent to it;

Nevertheless, He, the Exalted, took in His saying **وَلَا اللَّيْلُ سابِقُ النَّهارِ** absolute night and negated its precedence over absolute day. He did not say that one of the nights occurring in this order does not precede the day occurring in the order before it.

Thus, the judgment in the verse is based on what the nature of night and day requires according to the opposition that Allah has placed between them. From this, the judgment of the preservation of the order in the alternation of night and day has been derived, for every night is the absence of the day that it follows, so it does not precede it. To this, he (peace be upon him) points after mentioning the verse by his saying: "meaning the night has been preceded by the day," meaning that the day's preceding the night is its being created before it, and it is not as imagined that there are days and nights already existing and then a place is assigned for each of them.

As for the objector's saying: "As for calculation, it has a basis in general," it is not known why he said "in general," for it is a complete basis built upon the acceptance of the principles of astronomy, correct "in total" (بالجملة) on that assumption, not "in general" (في الجملة).

Likewise, his saying: "and the astronomers held that the beginning of the cycle is the meridian circle, which accords with what was mentioned," is of no avail. This is because the meridian circle—which is the circle passing through the two poles and a third point between them—is infinite in number and no specific point in the sky is assigned to it over another point, such that the sun being in one of them would be day for the earth and not the other.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّقُوا ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَما خَلْفَكُمْ**: Al-Halabi narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Its meaning is: Beware of what is before you of sins, and what is behind you of punishment."
